trick: Sounds exactly like English 'hallo' — you already know this one.
Think of 'choose' — you choose to leave, so you say Tschüss.
Think of a bitter taste — you say please with a bitter face when begging, and welcome with a kind face.
Think of a 'dance' — you dance with joy when someone helps you.
The longest apology in any language — you need a long word for a big sorry.
exampleTranslation: Excuse me, where is the train station?

trick: Morgen sounds like 'morning' — 'gut' means good, so Guten Morgen = Good Morning.
exampleSentence: Guten Morgen! Wie hast du geschlafen?
exampleTranslation: Good morning! How did you sleep?

trick: Abend sounds like 'absent' — the sun is absent in the evening.
exampleSentence: Guten Abend! Möchten Sie etwas trinken?
exampleTranslation: Good evening! Would you like something to drink?

trick: Wieder means 'again', sehen means 'see' — 'until we see each other again.'
Almost identical to English 'man' — a direct cognate.
Rhymes with 'frow' — imagine a woman throwing a frisbee (frow-ing it).
In English, 'kind' means nice — children are kind at heart.
exampleTranslation: The child is playing in the garden.

trick: Freund sounds like 'friend' — they share the same Germanic root.
Sounds almost identical to the Italian/Spanish 'famiglia/familia' — same Latin root as English 'family'.
In Yiddish (derived from German) 'mensch' means a good person — used in English too.
Sounds like 'J-mand' — some J-man (someone) is at the door.
exampleTranslation: Someone is knocking at the door.

trick: Nie means 'never' — niemand is the person who never exists (nobody).
Sounds like 'mutter' — mothers mutter quietly when we're in trouble.
Sounds like 'father' said with an accent — same Germanic root.
Sounds like 'brother' — almost the same word, same Germanic origin.
exampleSentence: Mein Bruder ist zehn Jahre alt.
exampleTranslation: My brother is ten years old.

trick: Schwester sounds like 'svester' — picture your sister in a vest (sweater-vest).
Short and sweet — 'Oma' is the universal grandma sound. Same in many languages.
Opa rhymes with Oma — they come as a pair, just like in real life.
Sounds like the French 'tante' and close to English 'aunt' — shared Latin root.
Sounds almost exactly like English 'uncle' — same word, essentially.
Same as 'null' in programming — zero, nothing, nada.
Think of Einstein — 'ein' means one, and he was the number one genius.
Sounds like 'sway' — two people swaying together on a dance floor.
Sounds like 'dry' — three-day drought leaves everything dry.
Sounds like 'fear' — four things that cause fear: spiders, heights, darkness, Mondays.
Fünf sounds like 'funf' — five is fun, full of adventure.
exampleSentence: Wir treffen uns um fünf Uhr.

trick: Sounds like 'sex' in some dialects — six is the naughty number.
Sounds like 'seven' said with a German accent — 'sieben' vs. 'seven'.
exampleTranslation: It is seven o'clock in the morning.

trick: Sounds like 'act' — on the count of eight, the actors take the stage.
exampleSentence: Der Zug kommt um acht Uhr.
exampleTranslation: The train arrives at eight o'clock.

trick: Sounds like 'noyn' — nine lives of a cat, now you know.
Sounds like 'tsane' — ten fingers on your hands.
Eleven = elf! A magical creature with eleven letters of mischief.
Twelve months in a year — zwölf Monate im Jahr.
exampleSentence: Es gibt zwölf Monate im Jahr.
exampleTranslation: There are twelve months in a year.

trick: Drei (3) + zehn (10) = 13 — German numbers are just math!
Vier (4) + zehn (10) = 14 — once you know the parts, the whole is easy.
Fünf (5) + zehn (10) = 15 — German compound numbers build themselves.
Sechs (6) + zehn (10) = 16.
Sieben (7) + zehn (10) = 17 — shortened to sieb-zehn.
exampleSentence: Es sind siebzehn Schüler in der Klasse.
exampleTranslation: There are seventeen students in the class.

trick: Acht (8) + zehn (10) = 18 — the age of adulthood in Germany.
Neun (9) + zehn (10) = 19 — one before twenty.
Zwei (2) + zig = 20 — 'zig' is the suffix for tens above twenty too.
Drei (3) + ßig = 30 — thirty is the dry-sig decade.
Vier (4) + zig = 40 — four tens make forty.
Fünf (5) + zig = 50 — fifty is the halfway point to a hundred.
Sounds like 'hundred' — same Germanic root, barely changed.
exampleTranslation: There is a hundred percent chance.

trick: Tausend sounds like 'thousand' said with a slight German accent — same ancient root.
Mon = Moon — Montag is Moon-day, just like Monday.
Dienst means 'service' — Tuesday is the day of service (originally Thing's day).
Mitt = middle, Woch = week — Wednesday is the MIDdle of the WEEk. Perfectly literal!
exampleSentence: Mittwoch ist die Mitte der Woche.
exampleTranslation: Wednesday is the middle of the week.

trick: Donner = thunder — Thor's day is Thunder-day, just like Thursday!
Frei = free — Friday feels free because the weekend is coming.
exampleSentence: Am Freitag gehe ich ins Kino.
exampleTranslation: On Friday I go to the cinema.

trick: Sounds like 'some-stag' — Saturday is some-day to do whatever you want.
Sonn = sun — Sunday is Sun-day, the day of the sun.
exampleSentence: Am Sonntag besuche ich meine Familie.
exampleTranslation: On Sunday I visit my family.

trick: Named after Janus, the two-faced Roman god — same in English and German.
exampleSentence: Im Januar ist es sehr kalt.
exampleTranslation: In January it is very cold.

trick: Same Latin root as English 'February' — sounds very similar.
exampleSentence: Im Februar gibt es oft Schnee.
exampleTranslation: In February there is often snow.

trick: Named after Mars, Roman god of war — marching soldiers in March.
Identical to English 'April' — no trick needed, it's the same word.
exampleTranslation: In April it rains a lot.

trick: Sounds like 'my' — May is MY favourite month (flowers everywhere).
Almost identical to 'June' — named after the goddess Juno.
exampleTranslation: In June the summer holidays begin.

trick: Almost identical to 'July' — named after Julius Caesar.
Identical to English 'August' — named after Emperor Augustus.
exampleSentence: Im August fahren wir in den Urlaub.
exampleTranslation: In August we go on holiday.

trick: Identical to English 'September' — septem is Latin for seven (it was the 7th month originally).
Same as English 'October' just spelled differently — octo is Latin for eight.
Identical to English 'November' — novem is Latin for nine.
Almost the same as English 'December' — decem is Latin for ten.
Früh means 'early' — spring is when things sprout early. Plus 'Früh' sounds like 'free' — spring feels free.
Almost identical to English 'summer' — same Germanic root.
Herbst sounds like 'harvest' — autumn is harvest time.
exampleSentence: Im Herbst werden die Blätter bunt.
exampleTranslation: In autumn the leaves turn colourful.

trick: Identical to English 'winter' — same ancient Germanic word.
Sounds like 'hoyday' — today is a hay-day, enjoy it!
Morgen sounds like 'morning' — tomorrow starts in the morning.
Ges-tern: imagine yesterday as a 'guest' (gest) who already left (tern away).
Jetzt sounds like 'yet-st' — are you doing it yet? Do it now!
Dann sounds like 'done' — first this, then (dann) the next thing, done!
exampleSentence: Erst lerne ich, dann spiele ich.
exampleTranslation: First I study, then I play.

trick: Sounds like 'spater' — you'll do it later, spater (greater) things await.
Früh sounds like 'free' — you feel free when you wake up early and have the whole day.
Spät sounds like 'späte' (late) — the spa closes spät (late) at night.
Immer sounds like 'I'm here' — always here, I'm always (immer) present.
Nie sounds like 'nee' — nee (born as) nothing, never existed.
Sounds like 'broat' — a sturdy loaf-shaped boat (a bROAT) sailing down a river.
exampleSentence: Ich kaufe das Brot beim Bäcker.
exampleTranslation: I buy the bread at the bakery.

trick: Wasser sounds like 'washer' — the washing machine uses lots of water.
Milch sounds almost like 'milk' — same Germanic root, practically the same word.
exampleTranslation: I drink milk in the mornings.

trick: Kaffee = coffee — borrowed from the same Turkish/Arabic root. Germans love their Kaffee.
Apfel is the ancestor of the English word 'apple' — German is older here.
Essen also means 'to eat' as a verb — das Essen is the noun form of eating.
Fleisch sounds like 'flesh' — meat is the flesh of an animal.
Fisch = fish — same Germanic word, almost no change over 2000 years.
Ge-mü-se: 'gee, muesli' — muesli has lots of plant-based ingredients like vegetables.
Obst sounds like 'obese' — eating too much fruit (sugar) can make you obese. Remember it by contrast.
Käse sounds like 'kasey' — Kasey the cheesy character from a cartoon.
exampleSentence: Der Käse aus der Schweiz ist gut.
exampleTranslation: The cheese from Switzerland is good.

trick: Ei sounds like 'eye' — eggs are round like eyes, looking at you.
exampleSentence: Ich esse ein Ei zum Frühstück.
exampleTranslation: I eat an egg for breakfast.

trick: Suppe sounds exactly like 'soup' with a German ending — same word essentially.
Salat = salad — same word, same Latin root (sal = salt).
Kuchen sounds like 'cookin' — the cake is still cooking in the oven.
Tee = tea — same word from Chinese via Dutch, shared across languages.
Saft sounds like 'saft' — as in 'sap' from a tree (tree juice).
Bier = beer — same word, same drink, same Germanic origin. Prost!
Wein sounds like 'wine' and also like 'vine' — wine comes from the vine!
Cola = cola — a global word, no memorization needed.
Glas = glass — same word, same object, same Germanic root.
Rot rhymes with 'hot' — red things are hot: fire, lava, the sun.
Blau sounds like 'blow' — blow a kiss to the blue sky.
Grün sounds like 'green' said with pursed lips — same Germanic root.
Gelb sounds like 'geld' — gold (Geld means money) is yellow-gold coloured.
Schwarz sounds like 'shvartz' — same root as Yiddish 'shvarts' meaning black.
Weiß sounds like 'vice' — white as the blank page of a vise-grip notebook.
Braun = brown — almost the same word, just spelled slightly differently.
Grau sounds like 'grow' — things grow grey with age.
Orange = orange — a truly international word, no memorization needed.
Lila = lilac/purple — the colour of lilac flowers (lila flowers).
Hemd sounds like 'hemmed' — shirts are hemmed at the edges.
exampleTranslation: I am wearing a white shirt.

trick: Hose sounds like 'hose' — trousers are like two hoses (leg tubes) attached together.
Kleid sounds like 'clad' — to be clad in a dress.
Jacke = jacket — same word with a German ending.
exampleSentence: Zieh die Jacke an, es ist kalt!
exampleTranslation: Put on your jacket, it's cold!

trick: Schuh = shoe — same sound! English borrowed 'shoe' from Germanic 'Schuh'.
Socke = sock — nearly identical, same Germanic root.
exampleTranslation: I am looking for my socks.

trick: Pullover = pullover — you pull it over your head. Exact same word!
Rock sounds like 'rock' — imagine a rock star in a skirt on stage.
exampleTranslation: She is wearing a red skirt.

trick: Mantel sounds like 'mantle' — a mantle is a cloak or coat.
exampleSentence: Im Winter trage ich einen Mantel.
exampleTranslation: In winter I wear a coat.

trick: Mütze sounds like 'mutts' — imagine a dog (mutt) wearing a silly hat.
exampleSentence: Trag eine Mütze, es ist kalt!

trick: Kopf sounds like 'cop' — a cop's head is always thinking about the law.
Hand = hand — exact same word! English and German share this ancient word.
Fuß sounds like 'foos' — your feet go foos-foos on the floor.
Auge sounds like 'ow-geh' — imagine poking your eye and saying 'OW!'
Ohr sounds like 'or' — 'or' do you hear with your ears?
Nase sounds like 'NASA' — astronauts in space don't use their nose (no smell in space).
Mund sounds like 'mund' in 'mundane' — eating mundane food with your mouth.
Arm = arm — identical word in English and German!
Bein sounds like 'bone' in dialect — your legs have your biggest bones.
Bauch sounds like 'bow' — take a bow with your belly (bauch) sticking out.
Haus = house — same Germanic root. Think of 'housing'.
Küche sounds like 'cuisine' — where all the cuisine is made.
Zimmer sounds like 'simmer' — things simmer in rooms (stew on the stove).
Stadt sounds like 'state' — a state has many cities.
Bahn = track, Hof = yard — the train yard (Bahnhof) is the train station.
exampleSentence: Der Bahnhof ist in der Stadtmitte.
exampleTranslation: The train station is in the city centre.

trick: Schule = school — same Latin root 'schola'. The word barely changed.
exampleSentence: Die Schule beginnt um acht Uhr.

trick: Markt = market — same word, same Latin root 'mercatus'.
exampleSentence: Ich kaufe Obst auf dem Markt.
exampleTranslation: I buy fruit at the market.

trick: Restaurant = restaurant — identical word from French, used worldwide.
Straße sounds like 'straight' — a straight stretch of road (street).
Büro = bureau — an office has a bureau (desk) in it.
Hotel = hotel — a truly international word, same everywhere.
exampleSentence: Das Hotel ist in der Nähe.

trick: Apotheke shares the root 'apothecary' — the old English word for pharmacist.
exampleSentence: Die Apotheke ist um die Ecke.
exampleTranslation: The pharmacy is around the corner.

trick: Auto = auto — short for automobile, same word worldwide.
Bus = bus — same word, short for omnibus in both languages.
exampleSentence: Der Bus kommt in fünf Minuten.
exampleTranslation: The bus comes in five minutes.

trick: Zug means 'pull' — a train pulls its carriages along the track.
Fahr = drive, Rad = wheel — a bicycle is literally a 'drive-wheel' in German.
exampleSentence: Ich fahre mit dem Fahrrad zur Schule.

trick: Flug = flight, Zeug = thing — a plane is literally a 'flight-thing'. German is wonderfully literal.
exampleSentence: Das Flugzeug fliegt nach New York.
exampleTranslation: The plane flies to New York.

trick: Straße (street) + Bahn (track) = Straßenbahn = street-track = tram. Perfectly logical!
Taxi = taxi — a universal word. Flag one down anywhere in the world.
exampleSentence: Ich nehme ein Taxi zum Hotel.
exampleTranslation: I'm taking a taxi to the hotel.

trick: Schiff = ship — same Germanic root. English 'ship' and German 'Schiff' are cousins.
Sein sounds like 'sane' — to be sane is to exist. Cogito ergo 'sein'.
Haven sounds like 'haven' — a haven is something you HAVE, a safe place.
Gehen sounds like 'gay-en' — going with energy and style.
Kommen sounds like 'come on' — someone's calling you to kommen!
Machen sounds like 'mocking' — making fun of something.
Sprechen sounds like 'sprech' — like 'speech', same Germanic root.
Essen sounds like 'essence' — eating is the essence of life.
Trinken sounds like 'drink-en' — almost the same word!
Heißen sounds like 'hissen' — your name is hoisted (hissen) like a flag.
Wohnen sounds like 'wonen' (Dutch for live) — where you live is where you're 'wone' to be.
'Arbeit' sounds like 'are-bite' — work means you 'are bitten' by responsibility.
Lernen sounds like 'learn-en' — to learn is to lernen, practically the same!
Schlafen sounds like 'schlaf' — imagine a sheep saying 'schlaf' as it falls asleep.
Kaufen sounds like 'coffen' — buying a coffin (coffen) — you need to buy one eventually.
Lesen sounds like 'lay-zen' — you lay down to read a book.
Schreiben sounds like 'scribe-en' — a scribe writes (schreibt) everything.
Hören sounds like 'hear-en' — to hören is to hear.
Sehen sounds like 'say-en' — 'I see what you're saying' — see and say are linked.
Kennen sounds like 'Ken' — you KNOW someone named Ken.
Möchten is the polite form of 'want' — when ordering in a café, always use möchten.
Können sounds like 'can-en' — to können is to can (be able to).
Müssen sounds like 'moosen' — you MUST go to the moose convention!
Sollen sounds like 'shall-en' — shall (should) you do it?
Dürfen sounds like 'daring' — you need permission to dare (dürfen) to do something.
Wollen sounds like 'volley' — you want to volley the ball back.
Groß sounds like 'gross' — something gross is always big and overwhelming.
Klein sounds like 'clean' — a clean minimalist room is klein (small and tidy).
Gut sounds like 'good' — same Germanic root, just simplified.
Schlecht sounds like 'slecht' (Dutch for bad) — bad and schlecht share Slavic/Germanic roots.
Neu sounds like 'new' — they are literally the same word from Proto-Germanic.
Alt = old — 'alt' in music means alto (older, lower voice). Alt in German means old.
Schön sounds like 'shown' — something shown (displayed) is meant to be beautiful.
Billig sounds like 'billing' — low billing (cheap) price.
Teuer sounds like 'tower' — luxury penthouses in towers are expensive.
Jung = young — same Germanic root. Jung and young are linguistic twins.
Schnell sounds like 'shell' — a shell fired from a gun goes fast.
Lang = long, sam = same — slow means a long journey that feels the same forever.
Warm = warm — identical word, same feeling.
Kalt sounds like 'cold' — same Germanic root, barely changed.
Lang = long — same Germanic root, same meaning, same sound.
Kurz sounds like 'curse' — a short, sharp curse word.
Schwer sounds like 'swear' — you swear when something is too heavy or difficult.
Leicht sounds like 'light' — something light is easy to carry.
Viel sounds like 'feel' — you feel it when there's too viel (much) of something.
exampleTranslation: I have a lot of work.

trick: Wenig sounds like 'waning' — the moon is waning, getting smaller (wenig = little).
Was sounds like English 'was' — but here it means 'what', not past tense of 'be'.
Wer sounds like 'where' — but it means 'who'. Wer ist dort? Who is there?
Wo sounds like 'woe' — 'oh woe, wo (where) did I leave my keys?'
Wann sounds like 'when' — almost the same word, same meaning!
Wie sounds like 'we' — how do WE do this together?
Warum sounds like 'vroom' — why (warum) does the car go vroom so fast?
Welche sounds like 'which' — same meaning, same Germanic root.
Wie (how) + viel (much) = wie viel (how much). German builds questions from parts.
Arzt sounds like 'artist' — doctors are artists of the body.
Lehrerin contains 'lehren' (to teach) — one who teaches is a Lehrerin.
Student = student — same Latin root 'studere', used internationally.
exampleSentence: Er ist Student an der Universität.
exampleTranslation: He is a student at the university.

trick: Kellner sounds like 'cellar' — waiters fetch wine from the cellar (Keller).
Verkäufer contains 'kaufen' (to buy) — a Verkäufer is someone who makes others buy.
Polizist = police + ist — a person who does police work. Clear enough!
exampleTranslation: The police officer helps the child.

trick: Ingenieur = engineer — from the French/Latin root 'ingenium', meaning cleverness.
exampleSentence: Der Ingenieur arbeitet an einem Projekt.
exampleTranslation: The engineer is working on a project.

trick: Koch sounds like 'cock' (rooster) — the rooster crows to wake the cook.
Mechaniker = mechanic — same Greek root 'mechane', used internationally.
exampleTranslation: The mechanic is repairing the car.

trick: Sekretärin = secretary — same Latin root 'secretarius', same meaning.
Arbeit sounds like 'are-bite' — work is when you 'are bitten' by duty.
exampleSentence: Die Arbeit beginnt um neun Uhr.

trick: Zeit sounds like 'site' — time is the site where all events happen.
Tag sounds like 'tag' — each day gets its own tag (label).
Jahr sounds like 'yar' — a pirate says 'yar, another Jahr has passed!'
Geld sounds like 'gilt' — gilt objects are covered in gold (money).
Name = name — same word, same Latin root, used worldwide.
Adresse = address — same word from French, used internationally.
Land = land — same word. A country is a stretch of land.
Sprache contains 'sprech' (speech) — a language is how people speak.
Frage sounds like 'fragile' — questions are fragile — handle with care.
